items.myth: Likelihood and probability are different formulas.
items.reality: They use the same PMF/PDF expression f(x | θ); only which variable is fixed (data vs parameter) and the question being asked differ.
items.example: Bernoulli PMF p^y(1−p)^{1−y} with y=1 fixed becomes L(p)=p—the likelihood curve over p, not a distribution over p.

items.myth: Likelihood values must lie between 0 and 1.
items.reality: Each factor f(x_i | θ) for a fixed θ is a valid probability or density in x, but L(θ) as a function of θ can exceed 1 and need not integrate to 1 over θ.
items.example: Gaussian PDF with small σ can give f(x|μ,σ) > 1 at the peak; L(μ,σ) inherits that when viewed as a function of μ and σ.

items.myth: Maximum likelihood gives the probability that θ is correct.
items.reality: MLE returns the parameter value that maximizes L(θ) for the observed data; uncertainty requires intervals, bootstrap, or a posterior—not the likelihood peak alone.
items.example: With 2 heads in 2 flips, MLE p̂=1, but that does not mean P(p=1|data)=1 without a prior and Bayes' rule.

items.reality: For Bernoulli and Categorical models, cross-entropy is negative log-likelihood; minimizing it is maximum likelihood under that model.
items.example: True class y with predicted π_y: loss −log π_y = −log L(θ) for that example's Categorical factor.
